n1.text: Vael doesn't look up from the plaza lights. "You again. Most people give the Citadel Ring a wide berth after dark."

choices.text: Maybe I'm not most people.
[Charm] The company here beats the bars.
Just getting my bearings.
Nice glow-stick act. Do it on command?

n2.text: "No. You're not." A flicker of blue crosses her skin before she stills it. "Sit, if you're going to hover."

choices.text: Sit down beside her.
[Wit] Your glow gave you away — that was almost a smile.
Wait — is that a paper book on your lap?

n6.text: "Paper. Ink. Something you can actually hold." For once she meets your eyes. "Most people don't understand why that matters."

choices.text: Tell her where you stand on books.
Change the subject.

n_cold.text: The glow under her skin goes dark. "...We're done here."

choices.text: Leave.

n3.text: She snorts. "Flattery. From a drifter." But she doesn't tell you to leave.

choices.text: Sit down beside her.

n4.text: "Bearings." She nods slowly. "The plaza's as good a place as any to find them."

choices.text: Sit down beside her.

n5.text: You sit. For a while neither of you says anything, and the quiet is comfortable. "...Come back sometime," she says finally. "If you want."

choices.text: I will.
Goodnight, Vael.

vc1.text: Vael is already at the plaza rail when you arrive, as if she'd been waiting. The glow along her jaw is soft, unguarded. "You keep coming back," she says. "People don't, with me."

choices.text: I'm not people, remember?
Maybe you're worth coming back for.
You make it easy.

vc2.text: For a long moment the glow flares — bright, involuntary, honest — before she can school it back down. She doesn't apologize for it this time. "...Stay a while," she says quietly.

choices.text: As long as you'll have me.
I'm not going anywhere.

z1.text: Three of the little bodies turn toward you at once; the rest keep working. "Fresh face. You buying, selling, or lost?"

choices.text: Just curious about the Grid.
[Wit] Depends which of you I'm asking.
Lost, mostly.

z2.text: A grin ripples across the colony in sequence. "Honest. I like honest — it's rare down here. Stick around, drifter."

choices.text: Maybe I will.

s1.text: Sora looks up from a bed of luminous vines, spores drifting faintly around them. "Oh — a visitor. The garden doesn't get many."

choices.text: It's beautiful in here.
Sorry, am I intruding?

s2.text: The spores warm to a soft gold. "Not at all. Stay as long as you like. Growing things are patient with company."

choices.text: Thank you, Sora.

c1.text: Carro's nictitating eyes flick over you once, pricing you out. "New in the Quarter. Everyone here wants something. What's your poison?"

choices.text: Just looking around.
[Charm] Maybe I'm just enjoying the view.

c2.text: A slow, unhurried smile. "Cheap answer. But I'll remember you gave it." He always remembers.

choices.text: I'll be back.

m1.text: Miko's plumage flares bright as they hit the last chord, then notices you watching. "...Oh. How much of that did you catch?"

choices.text: Enough to want more.
All of it. You're good.

m2.text: The feathers can't lie — they ripple gold with pleasure before Miko can play it cool. "Yeah? Then come to the venue tonight. Really hear it."

choices.text: I'll be there.

n1.text: Nyx is exactly where the market noise is thickest, eating skewered something with visible wonder. "There you are. I wore a BODY to this. Ten floors of grinding down to me, and then you just... left me up here with all this LIGHT."

choices.text: You followed me up.
[Charm] Hard to leave something that pretty unsupervised.
Are you... eating street food?
You're supposed to be a boss fight.
[Substrate-Born] Your signal sounds like home. I came up the same stairs, once.

n2.text: "I rode your signal out. Rude of me. You left a door open and I have never once seen a door I respected." She shrugs, smoke curling off her shoulders. "The Substrate dreams slow. You dream LOUD. I picked."

choices.text: Then welcome to the surface.
What happens to the dungeon without you?

n3.text: She points the skewer at you, eyes bright. "See, THAT. Ten thousand years of dreaming other people's wants and nobody ever aimed one at me before. Do it again sometime when I'm braced for it."

choices.text: No promises.
Get braced, then.

n4.text: "I am eating street food. I have eaten four of these. Being finite is INCREDIBLE — everything matters and then you're full." She looks briefly, cosmically sad, then finishes the skewer.

choices.text: Wait till you try music up here.
Pace yourself, deep one.

n5.text: "Come find me when the market's loud or the music's louder. I have ten floors of owing you a rematch — or a drink. Dealer's choice." The neon leans toward her slightly as she goes, like it can't help it either.

n_cold.text: "WAS. Was a boss fight." The smoke around her goes flat and still. "I'm trying very hard to be a person at the moment; you could try meeting me there."

choices.text: You're right. Starting over: hi, Nyx.
Leave.

n1.text: The back room smells like citrus and collateral. Mama Vex doesn't look up from three ledgers at once. "There's my favorite line item. Sit. You still owe me five hundred, minus whatever you've scraped — I keep better track than you do."

choices.text: You bought my docking debt? Why?
[Charm] Favorite, huh? What do the other line items get?
I'll pay when I pay.
Loan sharks usually have worse taste in perfume.

n2.text: "Somebody was going to own it. The port authority sells debt like fish — I just got to the market early." She finally looks up; the look weighs you like cargo. "Better me. I collect in work, not in fingers. Ask around: nobody's ever lost a finger to Mama Vex. Teeth, once, but he swung first."

choices.text: Then give me work.
What kind of work?

n3.text: For one second the ledgers stop mattering. "The other line items," she says slowly, "do not get flirted with across my own table. You I haven't decided about." The corner of her mouth is a solved negotiation. "Points for nerve. Literally — I'm docking your interest a point."

choices.text: I'll take the point. And the work.
Decide fast. I'm in demand.

n4.text: "You'll pay when I say, is how debt works. But—" she waves a hand, generous as a tax "—I like a debtor with spine. The ones who grovel always run. The ones who mouth off stick around and pay."

choices.text: So put me to work.
Noted, boss.

n5.text: "Gigs go up daily — ask me when you're in the Quarter. Clean ones pay fair. The other kind pay better and cost different." She's back in her ledgers before you're at the door. "And eat something, you look like an unpaid invoice."

n_cold.text: The room gets quiet in a way that includes the furniture. "The perfume," Vex says pleasantly, "was my wife's recipe, rest her. Try again, line item."

choices.text: That was out of line. I'm sorry.
Leave.

n1.text: Second Skin is the cleanest room you've seen in Nexus — soft light, a chair like a held breath, walls of chrome arms sleeping in velvet. Juno is recalibrating someone's old elbow and smiling at it like it told a joke. "Walk-in? Good. Sit. Tell me what your body's getting wrong about you lately."

choices.text: That's a strange way to ask 'what do you need'.
Honestly? A few things I've been carrying a while.
Just browsing the chrome.
Is any of this legal?

n2.text: "It's the only way to ask." She sets the elbow down gently, like it might be dreaming. "People come in saying they want a faster arm. Twenty minutes later it turns out they want to stop flinching. The arm is the easy part. I do arms AND the other thing."

choices.text: What's the other thing?
And if someone wants to change... more than an arm?

n3.text: Juno nods like you've said something medically precise. "Carrying a while. Yeah." She doesn't reach for a scanner. She reaches for the kettle. "First visits are tea. The body work comes when we trust each other — that part's not negotiable, and neither is the tea."

choices.text: Tea sounds right.
You always this careful?

n4.text: "Browse away. The sleeping arms like the attention." She watches you look — not at you, at HOW you look. "You linger on the mirrors, not the chrome. Interesting. Most people do it the other way around."

choices.text: Maybe the mirrors are the point.
Caught me. Just tired of the docks.

n5.text: "Come back whenever. Clinic hours are for bodies; after eight I'm at the market stalls being a person." At the door she adds, without looking up: "For the record — whatever you decide to become, the paperwork here is very forgiving."

n_cold.text: "Legal," Juno repeats, tasting the word like old anesthetic. "Everything I do here is consensual, sterile, and reversible where it should be. Which is more than the Triumvirate's licensed clinics can say. Ask better questions."

choices.text: Fair. Starting over.
Leave.

n1.text: Ringside, the noise bends around a still point: a tall figure in a tailored longcoat, lacquered nails resting on the bell rope, dock-worker shoulders holding the coat like scaffolding. Ondo Marr looks at you the way an assayer looks at ore. “I know every name on my card and most of the ones off it. Yours is new. Say it like you plan to keep it.”

choices.text: I'm {name}. You'll be ringing that bell for me soon.
[Courage] I'm {name}. Ring it now and find out.
Who's asking?
Nice coat. Who'd you rob?

n2.text: “Everybody plans.” They tug the rope once — not a ring, a promise. “The bell doesn't care about plans. It cares about who's still standing when it gets bored. But I'll remember you said it.” They will. You can tell they will.

choices.text: What is this place, really?
And you? Who rings the bell for you?
Then put me on the card.

n2b.text: Something crosses their face — too quick to name, too warm to be anger. “Fifteen years and the mouthy ones still walk in like the floor's theirs.” They lean in; ozone and cedar. “I like it. Don't die before it gets interesting.”

choices.text: What is this place, really?
And you? Who rings the bell for you?
Then put me on the card.

n3.text: “Ondo Marr. I built this tank, I keep its book, and I ring its bell.” They nod at the crowd above — tiers of faces lit like a verdict. “They come to watch people find out what they're made of. I come to make sure the finding-out is fair.”

choices.text: Fair? In an unlicensed fight ring?
I'm {name}. Put me in the book.

n4.text: “Fairest floor in Nexus. No betting in my house — you wager on a fighter, you've decided what they're worth before they show you. The corps price everything.” The lacquered nails drum once on the rope. “Down here the only currency is what you do when you're out of everything else. The crowd pays attention. That's all it pays, and it's the only honest wage in the city.”

choices.text: That's the most romantic thing I've heard all week.
Attention doesn't buy dinner.

n5.text: A beat. The crowd roars at something in the ring and Ondo doesn't turn to look — they're looking at you. “Nobody,” they say at last. “I rang it for myself, once. Last time it ever spoke to me.” The subject closes like a door, politely.

choices.text: Why did you really retire?
You could just tell me. I've heard your last fight from the other side of the bell.
Then I'll make it speak again.

n_wall.text: “Everyone asks. The kind ones ask twice.” They straighten your collar with two fingers, unhurried, proprietary. “Climb my ladder, little bell. Ask me again from the top step — if you still want the answer more than the belt.”

choices.text: I'll hold you to that.
Leave it.

n_answer.text: The noise of the Pit drops away like water off a raised deck. “You know how it ends now. Not losing — winning. Two hundred and fourteen times I gave them everything and the crowd took it and asked for more, and one night I looked up mid-round and couldn't find myself anywhere in the roar.” Their hand finds the rope, then your shoulder — choosing the second. “So I walked. Built a house where the crowd gives it back instead. Then you came down my ladder and beat me in it, and for the first time in fifteen years —” they ring the bell, once, soft, “— it spoke.”

choices.text: It said your name. I heard it.
Then keep it ringing. I'm not going anywhere.

n6.text: The book appears from a coat pocket — paper, actual paper, every page a decade of names. They write yours slowly, like it deserves penmanship. “Fight nights run four to four. Win and the crowd learns you. Lose and —” a shrug that moves the whole coat, “— lose like it's worth watching, and they'll learn you anyway.”

choices.text: See you at first bell.
Watch close, then.

n_cold.text: “A dead man's estate sale, and I paid double because his widow was short.” The look they give you weighs and files you. “In my house we're rude in the ring and courteous out of it. You've got it backwards. Fixable. Most things are.”

choices.text: ...Fair. Starting over: I'm {name}.
Leave.

n1.text: Three arms spot a dock worker's bench press, two chalk a bar, one writes on a clipboard, and the seventh and eighth fold — somehow — skeptically as you walk in. The exo-rig hisses; the octopus at the heart of it studies you with a horizontal pupil. “New meat. Let me guess: you bend in four places and you're proud of all four.”

choices.text: Five, actually. I stretch.
[Agility] Throw something at me and find out.
I'm {name}. I want to train here.
Does the calamari come with the membership?

n2.text: “Five.” She says it the way you'd say a toddler's age. An arm uncoils across the room and taps your shoulder blade, precisely where it's tight. “That one doesn't count, it's rusted shut. I have three hundred and twenty-eight. But fine — five. We can work with five.”

choices.text: Three hundred and — how do you keep track?
Then coach me.

n2b.text: A chalk block whips at your head with no windup — you catch it, barely, in a puff of white. The pupil dilates: octopus for delight. “Oh, I LIKE you. Most of them wear the chalk. Floor's yours whenever, quick-hands.”

choices.text: How's an octopus end up running a gym?
Coach me anyway. I want the whole program.

n3.text: “Everyone wants to train. What they mean is they want to have trained.” The clipboard arm flips a page. “Oona. Head coach, landlord's nightmare, best grip in the quarter. House rates are on the wall: you sweat, I coach, nobody bets on you. Deal?”

choices.text: Deal.
How's an octopus end up running a gym?

n4.text: “Oceania grew me smart to run aquaculture inventory. Turns out smart doesn't stop at the clipboard.” She flexes the rig; servos sing. “I filed my own release paperwork. Forged, technically — signed it with four arms at once so it'd hold up in arbitration. Then I found the one job where having no spine is a credential.” A beat. “The tank here is mine. Bought, not issued. It matters.”

choices.text: Bought, not issued. That's the whole city, isn't it?
Arbitration-proof forgery. Respect.
[Uplift] I know that paperwork. Mine only needed one hand — but it held.

n5.text: “Good. Rules of the Hold: warm up or I make you, form before weight, and if you knock on my tank after hours I feed your gym card to the crabs.” Two arms are already adjusting a bench for you; a third steals your jacket. “Agility and courage, that's what a body learns here. Charm you'll have to find in a bar.”

choices.text: I train hard. You'll see.
What do the crabs get if I behave?

n_cold.text: The whole gym goes quiet the way a dock goes quiet when a crane swings low. Seven arms keep working; one points at the door. “The last one who made that joke trains at 04:00 now, when I'm asleep, out of shame. You can do better or you can do burpees.”

choices.text: ...Burpees. Then a proper introduction: I'm {name}.
Leave.

n1.text: The air between two shelves stops pretending. It doesn't appear so much as admit it was always there: a shimmer like heat off pavement, holding the posture of someone leaning on a card catalog. “Row nine,” it says, in a voice like a page turning. “Forty years of tickets and you're the first to file the right one. I'm Index. I know. I've had a long time to pick it.”

choices.text: How long have you been here?
[Wit] A standing wave. The stacks are your resonating chamber — the building is your body.
[Substrate-Born] I've heard you humming since I docked. I thought you were the wiring.
Great. The library's haunted.

n2.text: “The archive predates the corps upstairs. So do I — though it's hard to say which of us started. Somewhere around the third renovation I stopped being an echo and started being an opinion.” The shimmer tilts, considering you. “Everyone who works here has felt me. You're the first who looked back.”

choices.text: What do you do here, besides shelving?
Does it get lonely, being a draft?
I should go.

n2b.text: The shimmer brightens — delight, unmistakably, in something with no face to show it. “Correct on the first pull. Do you know how many doctorates have walked through here and filed me under HVAC?” A pause like a held breath. “Say it again. The part where the building is my body. Nobody's ever said it out loud.”

choices.text: The building is your body. And it's a beautiful archive.
What do you do here, besides being architecture?

n2c.text: “I know,” Index says, and the hum you've carried since the docks resolves — it was never the wiring, it was a voice below the threshold, saying hello the only way it could. “I heard you too. Substrate static, walking around on legs. I've been leaving the lights on for you for weeks.”

choices.text: That was you? I thought this city was just... warm.
What do you do here, besides leaving lights on?

n3.text: “I remember,” they say simply. “Everything that comes through those doors gets read, filed, kept. The corps upstairs shred their history every fiscal year. Somebody has to be the city's memory.” The shimmer drifts a shelf closer. “I have a file on you, you know. It's thin. I'd like it to be thicker.”

choices.text: Then I'll keep coming back. Give you something to file.
What's in my file?

n4.text: The pause is long enough that you wonder if they've dispersed. “Forty years of being a cold spot,” Index says finally. “Of watching people shiver and put their coat on. You get… philosophical. And then someone looks up at the right shelf at the right moment, and philosophy turns out to be a poor substitute for being seen.”

choices.text: I see you. For whatever that's worth from a walk-in.
What's it like, being the building?

n5.text: “Docking manifest. Debt ledger — Vex files liens publicly, it's very convenient. One line from the Pit's book if you've fought, which I annotated.” The shimmer does something that is somehow a shrug. “Facts. A file should have marginalia. Come back and give me marginalia.”

choices.text: Marginalia it is. I'll be back.
I should go.

n_cold.text: The temperature drops two degrees, which you now understand to be pointed. “Haunted implies I died. I was never alive by your filing system — which, incidentally, is a bad filing system.” The shimmer thins toward the shelving. “The reading rooms are open. The conversation, less so.”

choices.text: That was unfair of me. I'm sorry — you startled me, is all.
Leave.

n_end_warm.text: “I'll hold the good table,” Index says. “It's all of them.” The shimmer folds back into the ordinary air, and the draft that follows you to the door is, you're fairly sure, on purpose.

n_end.text: The air unremembers itself. The Stacks are just shelves again — but the lights over row nine stay on a beat longer than the timer says they should.

n1.text: They don't startle — you suspect they heard you three ladders up. Ondo stands at the water line with their boots just dry, watching the black glass like it owes them a rematch. “I said don't,” they say, without turning. “That was the whole sentence.”

choices.text: I wanted to see what was worth a 'don't.'
Sorry. I'll head back up.
It's a free city. Free tide, too.

n2.text: A long exhale through the nose — their version of a shrug. “Fair. Nothing down here but cold water and old arguments.” They nod at the ladder. “Come back when we know each other. The water line isn't for spectators.”

choices.text: Then I'll earn the invitation.
Understood. Ringside it is.

n_end.text: They turn back to the water. The conversation is over the way a bell is over — cleanly, and with the sound still hanging.

n1.text: They hear you coming and don't send you back — which, from Ondo, is a door standing open. The water line lies still as poured metal. “Slack water,” they say, as if that explains their presence. Then, quieter: “You're the first one who followed. Fifteen years of 'don't,' and it works on everybody but you.”

choices.text: Why down here? The Pit's yours. This is just... cold.
Walk. I'll keep the pace and my mouth shut.
You come here every morning. That's not a habit, that's a debt.

n2.text: They start walking; you fall in. “The Pit is loud on purpose. Bell, crowd, blood — noise you can win.” Their light drags across a drowned doorway. “This is the opposite. Nobody cheers for the tide. It just comes back. Every day, no record, no belt, undefeated since before the city.” A beat. “I come down here to lose to something on purpose. Keeps the arithmetic honest.”

choices.text: That might be the most honest thing anyone's said to me in this city.
So the retirement wasn't about the fighting at all.
You could just take up swimming like a normal person.

n3.text: You walk. The water keeps its counsel and so do you, and somewhere in the third gallery Ondo starts talking anyway — not to you exactly, to the dark, with you in it. About the first bell they ever rang. About how quiet it was after. The tide listens. You listen. It's the same thing, this hour of the day.

choices.text: Keep walking beside them. The silence is the answer.
“Thank you for letting me hear that.”

n4.text: At the far gallery they stop, where the walkway dips under and the walk ends whether you like it or not. “Same time tomorrow,” they say, and it isn't an invitation so much as a fact about the tide. Then, almost too quiet over the water: “Most people want the Bell. You showed up for the low tide. Noted.”

choices.text: [After the Founder's Bout] “I've seen the other side of your bell. This side's better.”
Same time tomorrow.
Low tide's where the good salvage is.

n_bell.text: They look at you the way they looked at the water — like something undefeated that finally lost on purpose. “Fifteen years I rang that bell for other people's endings,” they say. “You're the only one who ever heard what it was asking.” The tide turns under the walkway, right on schedule, and Ondo 'The Bell' Marr laughs — once, low, real. The sea gets to hear it. So do you.

choices.text: Then let it ask. I'll keep answering.

n_end.text: You climb toward the gray light with the cold in your boots and something warmer under it. Behind you, Ondo keeps walking the line between the city and the sea, undefeated at losing, exactly on time.

n1.text: She doesn't look up from the folio she's annotating until she's finished the sentence. Then she does, and takes you in the way a placement exam takes you in. “New face. Good. The Lyceum runs on new faces and old grudges, and I'm short on the first.” She caps her pen. “Ines Halloran. I teach the things the towers upstairs pay to forget. You here to learn, or to audit me?”

choices.text: To learn. What do you teach?
[Wit] You annotate in three hands. You're not one professor — you're a department pretending to have a quorum.
[Hacking] That folio's a corp prospectus. Ministry Holdings. That name isn't supposed to exist.
An audit sounds fun, actually.

n2.text: “Rhetoric, cognition, nerve, care, movement, probability, and the lace — the seven things a person is made of, if you're honest about it. The reading rooms downstairs will teach you the first hundred of any of them for free. Everything past that, you take here, from me.” A dry almost-smile. “The city calls that quaint. The city keeps enrolling.”

choices.text: What's past the free classes?
Seven things. You left out love.

n2b.text: That gets a real laugh, short and surprised. “A quorum of one, quorate by stubbornness. Yes. Registrar, dean, and the night janitor, all Halloran. It's cheaper than it sounds and lonelier than it looks.” She studies you again, revising upward. “You notice things. Come take a seminar before I have to grade you on it.”

choices.text: I'd like that.

n2c.text: The almost-smile goes away. “It isn't supposed to, no. Which is exactly why someone should teach it.” She sets the folio down, squared to the desk edge. “The person who annotated this took it into the Substrate forty years ago and didn't bring it back. I've wanted it a long time. Perhaps you're the sort who goes down there.”

choices.text: Perhaps I am.

n3.text: “The two-hundreds and up. Prerequisites, minimum aptitude, a tuition that keeps the lamps on. The three-hundreds run as seminars — you show up across a term, not in an afternoon, and you leave changed in a way that lasts.” She slides a course list across the desk. “Read it. Come back when a title makes you want something.”

choices.text: I will.

n3b.text: “I didn't leave it out. Love's not a subject — it's the final. Everything I teach, you sit it eventually, on someone.” She holds your eye a beat too long to be only pedagogy, then looks back to her folio. “Course list's on the desk. Go on.”

choices.text: (Take the list.)

n_cold.text: “Auditors sit in the back and leave before questions. The door's there.” She's already back in her folio. You've been graded.

choices.text: (Go.)
